items.correct: Two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
items.misconception: A single melody with chord accompaniment.
items.why: Students confuse polyphony with thick texture or homophony. Polyphony specifically means INDEPENDENT MELODIES - like a fugue. Melody + chords is HOMOPHONY, not polyphony.

items.q: How many semitones are in a perfect fifth?
items.why: Students count fifth as 'five steps' but the name refers to letter NAMES (1-2-3-4-5), not semitones. C to G spans 5 letter names but 7 semitones (C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G).

items.correct: Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).
items.why: Students assume 'three sections' means three different ones. Ternary has only TWO distinct ideas, but A returns - it's the return that makes it ternary, not the section count alone.

items.q: One singer alone is what texture?
items.why: Correct answer: Monophonic. “Polyphonic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Pop song with singer + chords is what?
items.why: Correct answer: Homophonic. “Monophonic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many independent melodies make polyphonic texture (minimum)?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
